data.title: Why Goldman Sachs Stock Is Flat Despite Solid Early Trends In Earnings Growth
data.description:
In its first quarter (Q1) earnings, The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (NYSE:GS) - a global investment bank also deemed a “Global Systemically Important Bank” (G-SIB) - continues to retain its standing as the leader of the financial services sector so far this earnings season.

Overall line item trends indicate early indicators of outperformance relative to the previous Fiscal Year (FY) which, in itself, was a solid outperformer itself:

If trends are to continue, net earnings will be 36% higher than the previous FY's despite a comparatively modest 12% uptick in revenue along with an 8% increase in operating expenses.

When considering line item performance relative to revenue, a trend that's evident is that operating expenses relative to revenue have actually been falling since FY 2023.

As a result, net earnings (and profit margin) has been rising to the percentage-share highs seen in FY 2021. In fact, if trends were to continue, parity with the highs could possibly be attained by the end of FY 2025.

A persistent trend that was highlighted in the previous article about the bank's full year results persists in this quarter: deal-making (once a cornerstone of the bank's prowess in the industry) has shrunk away leading to market-facing segments such as Equities and FICC (“Fixed Income, Currency & Commodities”) as well as client wealth management accounting for 75% of the total revenue.

Quick Note: “net revenue” or “revenue” is used in place of what banks refer to as “Revenue Before Provision for Loan Losses.” With more market-related activities, it is likely that this provisioning becomes even more pertinent.

Moreover, provision for credit losses went from $351 million in Q4 2024 to $287 million in Q1 2025, suggesting an overall improvement in business operations.

However, in a statement released on the same day as the earnings release, CEO David Solomon stated that "we are entering the second quarter with a markedly different operating environment than earlier this year" - a warning that Q2 onwards might need a certain grounding of expectations.

Recession Worries Deepen

CEO Solomon wasn't the only major financial services leader to essentially issue a warning about the impact of the ongoing tariff war. JPMorgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon stated that he believes its more likely than not that were will be a recession in the present year while BlackRock (BLK) CEO Larry Fink warned that the impact of the tariff war could be widespread.

Note: The tariff war - and the likely drivers necessitating it - was covered in detail in an earlier article.

As a result of market uncertainty over the impact of tariffs on consumption, inflation and the markets, long-term borrowing rates have dropped, making it harder for banks to realize sizeable profits on loans. In addition, deal-making divisions are expected to take a hit as mergers & acquisitions (M&A) and IPO plans are shelved.

As it stands, a drop in deal-making isn't a massive jolt given its reduced role but it is still a net contributor to the bottom line. The ongoing tussle between China and the U.S. is steadily growing deeper, with the government of the former now directing its airlines to not take deliveries of Boeing jets and associated aircraft parts from U.S. suppliers. This marks another part of Goldman Sachs' vulnerability: about a steady 11% of its revenue has been originating from Asia for a few years now - with a substantial portion likely with some connection to China, either directly or indirectly. If the Chinese government were to go on to order restrictions on relationships with American financial institutions, there is a possibility that this will be yet another blow on revenue growth.

In fact, CEO Solomon had explicitly stated that President Trump's policies "reset the prospect of forward growth pretty significantly all over the world."

In Conclusion

As markets tumble, the prospect of a rate cut to spur lending activities is inevitable. While lower rates means lower profit booking, the spurring of economic activity might net banks greater revenue from a wider base of clients - which is why financials have now tended to perk up in conviction during periods of stronger recessionary outlook.

Despite CEO Solomon's warnings about the forward outlook, the stock did rise by a little under 2% in the course of the trading session on the 14th of April. While after-hours trading and pre-market trading session hinted at an erasure of these gains as a result of intensifying trade disputes, it's more likely than not that the stock prices of the likes of Goldman Sachs would far less badly than most other sectors.

Given that the bank also issues dividends (and had, in fact, authorized a respectable $3 quarterly dividend), there is some strength to the argument that holding a position in the bank's stock would do well for portfolio diversification in the year to come.

data.title: SkyWest: The Best Airline Stock Heading Into Earnings
data.description:
In November, I covered SkyWest, Inc. (NASDAQ:SKYW) with a strong buy rating. The stock price performance, however, has not been satisfactory with a 15% decline, underperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which lost 5.6%. The stock price is still up 80% from my buy call in November 2023 compared to a 18.9% return for the S&P 500. So, the stock price decline since November is not dramatic.

In this report, I will discuss what I like about SkyWest and I will discuss the company’s upcoming earnings. Additionally, I will update my stock price target for the company.

SkyWest Business Is Shielded Very Well

Airlines are generally exposed to macroeconomic events that affect costs as well as unit revenues. That is also why we recently saw Delta (DAL) and Frontier Airlines (ULCC) pull their full year guidance.

SkyWest, however, is to a major extent isolated from these fluctuations. The company operates under capacity agreements which include minimum guarantees. So, whether base fares are high or low there is no revenue impact on the revenues. Even in times of economic weakening, the business is shielded through minimum guarantees set out in capacity agreements with Delta, United (UAL), American Airlines (AAL) and Alaska Airlines (ALK).

Similarly, fuel costs can be passed through to the parties for which SkyWest operates the capacity agreements.

The business, however, is not fully isolated from market dynamics. Prorate agreements and charter flights represent 13% of revenues. The prorate agreements allow SkyWest to share in the base fares and expose the regional airline to virtually all costs. Similarly, on charter flights the company is also exposed to the market dynamics.

The reason why SkyWest stock may have sold off recently is because there could be a fear that the mainline carriers will require less regional capacity flying if market conditions soften further and it is probably also a sentiment issue. Usually, when stocks in a sector or industry decline or gain there is little regard for whether the dynamics of a specific subset of companies are materially different from the peer group and as a result potentially could offer a better risk profile.

What Are Analysts Expecting For The First Quarter Earnings?

For the first quarter (expected April 24th), analysts expect SkyWest to report revenues of $946.9 million indicating 17.8% year-on-year growth. That should translate into earnings per share of $2.05, which indicates 41% growth. The company expects block hour execution to be up 12% in 2025 providing support to the higher revenues in addition to increased unit rates.

The company has not guided for EPS, but it shared during the fourth quarter earnings call that its EPS could be around $9 per share for the full year. The first quarter tends to be the weakest quarter so a $2.05 earnings per share would be quite a solid start. The significant growth in EPS should really come from growing the top line while keeping costs growth low.

For the first quarter, over the past six months, the EPS and revenue revisions have been positive with revenue estimates improving 3.4% and EPS estimates revisions up 2.9%. Those are not huge changes, but they are positive.

The company has a good history on beating analyst revenue and EPS estimates. Over the past two years, the company beat EPS estimates each quarter and only produced one miss on revenues, which was in Q4 2023 when it missed estimates by 0.9%. In all other quarters revenue beats ranged between 1.3% and 4.9%.

During the first quarter call, I hope that SkyWest management will be able to reconfirm and highlight its shielding from weakening in air fares and provide some insights on how the current market turmoil is affecting demand for regional jet operations if there is any impact.

Strong Buy Rating Reiterated For SkyWest

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For SkyWest, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating with a $126.98 price target providing 49% upside. I have lifted my price target by around $7 per share. The valuation is based on 2024 earnings in line with a peer group EV/EBITDA multiple. This means that we are not factoring in the premium that SkyWest usually trades at nor are we incorporating the upside driven by growth in the years ahead. Putting it very simple, at this very moment, the stock price should already be at $127.

EBITDA is set to grow at a rate of 20.6% annually through 2027. Free cash flow growth is expected to be8% annually but we do note that free cash flow in 2024 was around 25% weaker than expected and the estimate for 2025 has also come down by around 25%. So, the free cash flow is definitely a watch item.

Conclusion: SkyWest Shielded Business Is Underappreciated

I believe that SkyWest stock is currently significantly undervalued. The company has some exposure to fluctuations in base fares and fuel costs through its prorate agreements and charter flights. These accounted for around 13% of the revenues in 2024.

However, the company is generally shielded very well from fluctuations in revenue and cost dynamics through capacity agreements that also provide minimum guarantees. I believe that this is not reflected in the current stock price nor is the strong execution in 2024. As a result, I maintain my strong buy rating and increase my price target to $126.98.

data.title: Riverwater Partners Small-Cap Q1 2025 Letter
data.description:
Small-cap stocks took a step back in this quarter. This followed strong momentum after the November election. Tariff announcements created significant business uncertainty as they flipped between on again off again tariff announcements with two of our largest trading partners, Canada and Mexico. The current administration’s tariff policies differ in two ways from the last tariff policy initiations in 2018. First, the previous round of tariffs came after significant corporate tax cuts. Second, the last round was focused on China and not Canada, Mexico, and the rest of the world.

Canada and Mexico are intricately enmeshed in our supply chains. We have had the largest free trade zone in the world for over 30 years with NAFTA and then its successor, the USMCA. Businesses gained confidence to allocate capital in both Canada and Mexico thanks to these agreements. Business sentiment surveys are already reflecting sudden uncertainty around these trade relationships.

We own companies across our strategies that either sell outside the US or have supply chains outside the US. After completing a thorough review for every holding, we are comfortable with our current positioning and believe we have lower exposures than our benchmarks. However, it can be difficult to assess benchmark weights as there is no aggregate data available for exposures by country.

Government Spending and Fiscal Policy

Fiscal policy shifts are adding another layer of uncertainty to the economic outlook. The new administration’s emphasis on spending cuts coincides with the final phaseout of pandemic-era stimulus measures. This will likely slow economic growth in the short term.

President Trump’s recent decision to eliminate the penny is symbolic. It shows how cost-cutting policies can have hidden consequences. Like the penny—which costs 3.7¢ to mint but is worth only 1¢—many federal programs are being evaluated for their immediate cost rather than their broader economic impact. This mindset echoes the administration’s tariff approach: headline-driven policies that overlook supply chain complexity. For example, it’s estimated the average American-made car could cost $3,500 to $10,000 or more under the proposed tariffs.

Eliminating the penny may seem like a cost-saving measure, but it raises further questions. The nickel, which costs 13.8¢ to produce versus its 5¢ face value, results in an even greater loss—8.3¢ per coin. If more nickels are minted to offset the penny loss, the US Mint’s net losses could increase. And removing both coins would force retailers to round prices, potentially pushing them higher and contributing to inflation. Tariffs could have a similar effect: narrow, targeted measures that lead to broad and compounding economic consequences as trade partners retaliate. Tariff announcements are coming in as we write, and clearly the market has reacted negatively to both US tariffs and affected countries' counter tariffs.

As we look to add new companies to our portfolios, we are certainly cognizant of companies’ supply chain dependencies and foreign sales exposures. We’re likely to avoid businesses vulnerable to an extended tariff war. Fortunately, we believe small-caps are better positioned than large caps as it relates to tariff exposures because of their domestic orientation.

Valuation: The Small-cap Opportunity

Investors pulled back from the Russell 2000 in Q1 2025, driving a 9.5% decline and underperformance versus large caps, with the S&P 500 declining 4.3% over the same period. This reversal highlights small-cap volatility in response to policy shifts. While the media focuses obsessively on the “Magnificent Seven,” small-caps are quietly trading below their historical valuation averages. Using the Shiller CAPE (cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings) methodology, which smooths earnings over a ten-year period to temper the effects of earnings booms and busts, we find compelling evidence that small caps offer significant value. Thanks, as always, to our friends at Furey Research Partners for actually creating a CAPE for the small-cap market. Analysis of Furey’s work on the Russell 2000 CAPE ratio reveals:

  • Small-cap CAPE valuation is trading below its long-term average at 37.5x
  • Large-cap CAPE valuation is near one standard deviation above its long-term average
  • Small vs. large relative CAPE valuation is near all-time lows
  • Historical data suggests small-caps may deliver approximately 8.0% annualized returns over the next decade from current levels, compared to just 3.5% for the S&P 500

This significant valuation discount suggests that despite near-term challenges, the stage is being set for potential small-cap outperformance over the long term. The last time small caps traded at such a significant discount to large caps was in the late 1990s, which was followed by several years of substantial outperformance.

Looking Ahead: Navigating the Changing Landscape

Despite the challenging quarter, two potential catalysts could drive small-cap outperformance as 2025 progresses. First, a resolution of tariff and fiscal uncertainties. Second, valuation recognition: the substantial discount of small-cap valuations compared to large may finally attract increased investor attention, especially if large cap growth begins to slow. Valuations are never a catalyst in themselves, but they could certainly add fuel to the fire if they do take the mantle of leadership.

Conclusion

While small-caps face real challenges in the current environment, history suggests that periods of significant underperformance and valuation discounts often present the most attractive entry points for long-term investors. The Russell 2000’s current CAPE ratio – well below historical averages – provides a substantial margin of safety that should help mitigate downside risks while positioning for future appreciation.

For investors with appropriate time horizons, the current environment may offer an opportunity to increase small-cap exposure at valuations that have historically preceded periods of outperformance. While near-term volatility is likely to persist until tariff and fiscal policies stabilize, the compelling valuation case for small caps continues to strengthen.

Adam J. Peck, CFA, Founder and CIO

The information contained herein represents the opinion of Riverwater and should not be construed as personalized or individualized investment advice. Analysis and opinion expressed in this report are subject to change without notice. The securities identified and described do not represent all of the securities purchased, sold or recommended for client accounts. The reader should not assume that an investment in the securities identified was or will be profitable

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JEPI: A Top 8%-Yielding Covered Call ETF
data.description:
The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) is a promising covered call ETF whose forward dividend yield has spiked lately as markets sold off and investors reacted in a panicky way to new U.S. tariffs. The ETF provides income-focused investors with an opportunity to own a portfolio comprised of growing, dividend-paying stocks (equity exposure) while the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF also sells call options in order to generate portfolio income. The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF pays shareholders an 8% yield which recently surged amid panic selling in the market. I believe the drop is a great opportunity to load up on a well-run covered call ETF that pays investors a monthly dividend.

Previous rating

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF was a buy -- 7% Yield, Diversified, IT Exposure -- for me in my last coverage in October 2024 which is when I called JEPI a promising ETF play for its defensive equity positioning. In my view, the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF is a potentially attractive income investment on the drop as the ETF's yield has risen above its longer term average. With markets panicking over new U.S. tariffs, especially with respect to China, I believe buying JEPI could make sense for investors that look for an attractive entry point into a leading covered call ETF.

Defensive equity portfolio, call option strategy to generate income

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF takes equity positions in leading U.S. companies such as Visa (V), Mastercard, McDonald’s (MCD), Microsoft (MSFT) or Amazon (AMZN) and currently is one of the largest covered call ETFs in the market: the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF manages approximately $37.4B in net assets which the ETF has accumulated in a relatively short period of time. The ETF was incepted only on May 20, 2020 and is therefore looking back on a relatively short performance track record.

The covered call ETF combines defensive equity exposure with an option call strategy which the ETF uses to generate recurring monthly distributions for shareholders.

Currently, the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF has as its largest portfolio holding Visa (V) which represents about 1.7% of the ETF's total value, which is followed by Progressive (PGR) and Mastercard (MA) that have similar weightings of 1.7% and 1.6%. The ETF is overall very well diversified with its top ten investments representing only 15.3% of all investments. The largest industry is Information Technology which represented approximately 14.7% of all fund investments.

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF has a solid return history since its inception in 2020: since its inception the ETF has delivered an annual NAV return of 11.84%. The ETF's year-to-date return of 0.40% is very weak, however, but relates back to the massive market swings we have seen in recent weeks that relate back to growing investor uncertainty about U.S. tariffs.

JEPI's yield has risen above the 1-year average, indicating undervaluation

Because of tariff woes in the market, the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF's dividend yield has spiked above 8% lately, indicating that investors may have adopted too bearish an outlook on equities in general. The 3-year average dividend yield for the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF is 6.89%, so investors could be looking at an attractive entry point into the ETF at this point in time, in my opinion.

JEPI’s valuation

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF published a net asset value of $54.04 per-share on Friday which compares against a market ETF price of $53.98 per-share. The valuation now reflects a 0.11% discount to net asset value which is also below the 3-year average of a 0.04% premium. While the undervalued is not as excessive as it was back in 2022, both the dividend yield and the NAV discount indicate that this could be a good point to build a position in the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF.

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF has a relatively short investment history, but the ETF has managed to grow its net asset value and has been a solid distribution play for investors, especially those that want to leverage an equity portfolio to generate recurring income. As long as the U.S. economy is on an upward trajectory, I believe the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF will remain an interesting vehicle for dividend investors.

Risks with JEPI

The biggest risk for the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF, as I see it, relates to the company’s long equity exposure. With trade tensions escalating recently and investors fearful of a recession, even defensively-oriented ETFs could see valuation draw-downs. A red flag would likely also be if the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF lost fund assets or if the ETF were to see a decline in its monthly distributions.

Final thoughts

The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF is a promising covered call ETF that I have slowly been dollar cost-averaging into in the last week. The main appeal of the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF is that the ETF offers exposure to large-cap U.S. companies that have a history of demonstrated earnings growth.

Additionally, the ETF monetizes this equity exposure through the application of a call option strategy which generates recurring dividend income for the ETF. As the markets tanked last week on concerns over new U.S. tariffs, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF's dividend yield spiked and rose above its longer term average while at the same time the ETF's shares started to trade at a discount to NAV... potentially signaling undervaluation.

I believe that the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF has the potential to deliver strong dividend income for many years to come and with the portfolio being as well-diversified as it is, I believe the downside risk here is pretty much limited. Investors can grab an 8% dividend yield out of the bargain bin again and I see a favorable long term risk profile for dividend investors.

data.title: Royal Gold Has Good Business Model But It Should Underperform Peers
data.description:
Introduction

While the stock market is facing quite a volatile environment, gold has lived up to its tag as a safe haven. The commodity has gained ~38% in the last 12 months while the broader market has struggled to post single-digit returns.

The rally in gold has put many of the gold mining companies into a sweet spot, attracting attention from retail and institutional investors alike. RGLD is one of the gold mining companies that have benefited from this strong undercurrent, posting strong gains from the start of this year. While I like RGLD as a business with a safer cost structure than other gold miners and good growth prospects, I believe the stock is valued quite richly. Moreover, given RGLD’s lower and variable cost structure, it makes its P&L have lower operating leverage, leading to relative underperformance to other miners in times of surging gold prices. Given these reasons, I would like to sit on the sidelines for the time being for RGLD.

A double beat

Back in February, RGLD came out with impressive results, with both its topline and bottom line comfortably beating the consensus estimates, primarily driven by strong gold sales that came in at the higher end of the guidance range as well as better gold pricing. There was a strong contribution from assets like Peñasquito, Manh Choh, Bellevue and Robinson on the royalty segment front, while for the stream segment, properties such as Mount Milligan, Rainy River, Pueblo Viejo, and Wassa contributed to increased growth. Moreover, the commencement of new assets such as Rosa, Manh Choh, and Côté Gold and an earlier than expected gold delivery from the Andacollo asset contributed to the higher metal production.

On the flip side, the silver sales were slightly lower than the actual guided range, primarily due to lower recovery from the Pueblo Viejo asset. Furthermore, the Pueblo Viejo asset should also witness a 35-day shutdown in the upcoming quarter, which should impact deliveries and sales with a 3-5 month lag. To put this into perspective, Pueblo Viejo is the third-largest asset for RGLD, with a ~13% contribution to the overall revenue.

Royal Gold has outperformed gold unlike most of the miners

During the dull period of 2011-2020, when gold prices went nowhere but sideways, it was actually RGLD that outperformed the underlying metal prices. Moreover, during that period the gold miners’ index (GDX) turned out to be a wealth destructor for the period.

This outperformance is deeply rooted in the business model of RGLD. Unlike other gold miners, which have operational expenses to produce gold from mines and pocket the difference between spot gold prices and the cost of mining, RGLD has a business like that of an investment company that finances these mining projects in exchange for some or the full portion of the production at a discounted price to the spot market, or it can also structure this deal into a royalty payment structure, hence the segments (stream segment and royalty segment).

This business model helps RGLD to participate in the gold market while also limiting its risk on the operational front. While this business model has a somewhat limited downside for RGLD, it also has some drawbacks relatable to the current scenario.

Since RGLD does not share most of the operational cost, its margins are very high compared to other miners that have operational cost structure in their P&L. This benefit inherently reduces the operating leverage for RGLD, resulting in comparatively lower growth in the bottom line in case of a higher gold price as compared to miners who have fixed operational costs in their P&L. Hence, in the current gold price surge, I believe RGLD should underperform conventional miners based on this dynamic. However, it's much safer than the other miners in case of a gold price decline.

Growth from key assets to benefit Royal gold

Going forward, RGLD’s management has guided for somewhat flat volume growth for the company in terms of gold and a drop in silver guidance compared to guidance given for 2024.

Beginning with the largest asset of the RGLD Mount Milligan mining complex, which contributes ~26% of the production for RGLD and is operated by Centerra Gold. According to the operator, the production is expected to be marginally up for gold (165-185K oz as compared to 167K oz in 2024) as well as copper (50-60 million pounds as compared to 54 million pounds in 2024). Moreover, Centerra is also assessing a preliminary economic assessment (PEA) to extend mine life beyond 2035, which is expected to be completed by the first half of 2025.

Moving on to the Cortez mining complex, which happens to be the second-largest asset for RGLD by revenue, moreover, this unit is a royalty-based asset, which tends to have higher profitability than the stream assets. This asset has one of the highest potentials to grow RGLD’s bottom line going forward given key growth initiatives taken by its operator [Nevada Gold Mines, a JV between Barrick Gold (GOLD) and Newmont (NEM)]. Goldrush Mine in the Cortez mining complex, which is currently being ramped up, would eventually grow to 400K oz of gold per year by 2028. The current rate of production from this mine is ~130K oz in 2024. This mine would be the key driver for production growth from the Cortez mining complex, which is forecasted to reach 1 million oz by 2027 (4Q24). With regard to longer-term potential at Cortez, Barrick has reported a significant increase in gold resources at the Fourmile project. According to the PEA, the Fourmile project indicates a potential for gold production levels exceeding 500K oz per year. The pre-feasibility study for these projects should begin in 2025. Once in operation, this site should be a significant growth driver for the company.

Coming to the third-largest asset of RGLD, Pueblo Viejo. Although the mining complex had disappointing silver recovery for the last few quarters, its operator (Barrick) is taking measures to increase throughput to ~14 million tonnes per year by 2028. As part of these measures, the operator has noted a 35-day impact to production for the first quarter.

Valuation and Conclusion

Given RGLD’s resilient cost structure as compared to gold miners, growth runway ahead and surging gold prices, the company has been richly valued. If we look at Seeking Alpha's Quant valuation rating, the company is rated an F. On looking at the price-to-earnings ratio, the company looks quite expensive, trading at a 27.9x forward PE ratio.

If we look at the price-to-book ratio, the company looks quite expensive here too, with a ~3.78x multiple, which is near the higher end of the historical range.

Although gold prices are in favor of RGLD, I believe RGLD would benefit relatively less than other gold miners that have higher operating leverage. While I like RGLD as a business, its stock is dearly priced. That, combined with lower operating leverage compared to other miners, makes me believe I should pass on this stock for now. Hence, I have a hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Cameco Corp: Unrivaled Western Uranium Hegemony From Mines To Micro Reactors Edge
data.description:
Investment thesis

I am bullish on (NYSE: NYSE:CCJ) from the uranium production angle, where the company plans to produce over 22.4 million pounds of Uranium in 2025.

By looking at the production of Uranium by Cameco, I am convinced that despite the reduced Uranium production guidance from a total of 23.4 million pounds in 2024 to 22.4 million pounds in 2025 from its major production mines (McArthur River/Key Lake and Cigar Lake), the company has a high bullish potential. The company has executed varied contracts that position it to sell over 220 million pounds to 41 customers globally. However, the western region, America at 44% and Europe at 39%, takes the largest share of its customers, 83%. With only a small customer base in Asia at 17% and adding the ban on Russian uranium imports into the US in the picture, Cameco has a high bullish potential from Western dominance. At the new record high uranium production hitting 20.3 million at McArthur/Key Lake, surpassing any global mining operation, Cameco is best positioned to supply the most Uranium to Western utilities. Digitization and automation optimization of projects at the Key Lake have played a key role in this success.

I am also considering Cameco's edge in uranium production and its commitment to exploring reactor and fuel supply. This adds an aspect of engineering, operating, and maintaining the development of new reactors. At the moment, in terms of Uranium production, there are only 3 entities that match Cameco production in the western region. Its competitors in terms of Uranium production are Russian Rosatom, which has been barred under the Russian uranium import ban, China Atomic Authority, and French Orano. Orano faced challenges at their SOMAIR mine in Niger following the coup d’état in July 2023, and China Atomic Authority faced Tariffs and other geopolitics challenges that best positioned Cameco for the western region market. I have explained in more detail my basis on why Cameco is bullish, looking at its uranium production capacity and its market dominance in uranium supply in the western region.

Company brief

Cameco is a uranium production company based in Saskatoon, Canada. Uranium production is mainly used to generate electricity. The company operates three segments: Fuel Services, Uranium, and Westinghouse. The uranium segment focuses on mining, milling, and purchasing and selling Uranium. The Fuel Services segment converts, refines, and fabricates uranium concentrate, purchases, and conversion services. Westinghouse segment operates nuclear reactor technology and originally manufactured equipment and provides products and services to government agencies and commercial utilities. The company also provides engineering services around nuclear utilities, such as instrumentation, maintenance, plant modification, and engineering support.

Cameco has an edge in Uranium market growth

Let's first look at the global perspective of nuclear energy demand and later market Cameco, which is dominating. On a global scale, the demand for nuclear power in 2024 was USD 435.54 giga-watts. In 2025, nuclear power is estimated to hit 2.76tn kWh globally at a CAGR of 1.56% between 2025 to 2029. This increase is driven by the increased uptake of energy-sustainable generation sources, leading to the nuclear sector regaining its interest due to the low carbon it emits.

While China is leading in the number of installed large-scale reactors, Cameco still has 17% sales in Asia.

In Eastern Europe and the UK, large-scale reactors have declined from 34% in the 1990s to 23%, necessitated by running and maintenance costs. This decline is matched with efficient, cost-effective nuclear energy models known as SMRs, the new generation of reactors. SMRs are the new catalysts of nuclear energy, and the plan is to marry maturity up to 25GW capacity.

The growth of nuclear power adoption by data centers is still solid, going by the projected SMR capacity to hit 40 GW by 2050. The United States is leading in SMR research at 35%, and Europe is expected to account for 15% between 2025 and 2030. The growth of nuclear energy driven by SMRs and varied advanced nuclear reactors plays a key role in low carbon energy supply, which is an added advantage. For example, in the northeast of the United States, data centers are driving electricity demand. On the other hand, in Europe, electricity demand is accelerating rapidly, and a case in point shows that in 2022, 17% of Ireland’s total electricity was used on data centers. This consumption in Ireland is expected to double by 2026.

Based on my thoughts above, my pick on the western uranium market as a solid market for Cameco is based on the projected SMR installed capacity between 2025 and 2040.

From this take on the SMR evolution of uranium usefulness, I hope you note that since uranium production is the core of the Cameca business, it has an ample productive capacity to meet the demand of expanding nuclear energy and fuel needs. For example, Tier-one operations in the McArthur River mine/Key Lake mill have estimated reserves of 251.0 M lb in its estimated mine life by 2044, when the SMRs are expected to skyrocket.

Clearly, Cameco is prepared to meet the demand for uranium projected by the SMR revolution and other nuclear reactor technologies. This goes with Cameca Management Discussion and Analysis forward-looking statement on SMR to participate in SMR commercialization and deployment among other fuel cycle value chains.

Financials: Annual and Q4 highlights shows optimistic signs

The company has strong financials, with annual net earnings hitting $172 million after cash from operations generating $905 million. This delivered an adjusted EBITDA increase of 73% from $884 million in 2023 to $1.5 billion in 2024. Westinghouse recorded an adjusted EBITDA of $483 million from $101 million in 2023. However, the full year recorded a net loss of $218 million attributed to inventories revaluation on market prices compared to the acquisition time.

For the quarter, the company recorded net earnings of $135 million, an improvement from $80 million in net earnings in Q4’23. This delivered an increased adjusted net earnings in the quarter from $90 million in Q4’23 to $157 million adjusted net earnings.

The company continued to generate value for its shareholders with an increased annual dividend at $0.16 per common share in 2024 from $0.12 per common share in 2023. From the company's promising business, it expects an increase of $0.4 per common share in 2025 and 2026, achieving a double-digit dividend in 2023 from $0.16 to $0.24 in 2026 per common share.

Analysts estimate the Cameca projection of double-digit EPS of $0.24 in 2026 to be achieved in the next quarter, June 2025, and the yearly EPS will hit between $1.26 and $1.7. Going by the near-term risks such as geopolitical issues, the company may likely not achieve the analyst's estimates, but it is poised for this performance in a perfect or ideal business environment.

The company also has a strong balance sheet with available cash liquidity of $600 million and a $1 billion undrawn credit facility. The prices of Uranium are forecasted to rebound mid-2025 to around $90-$100 per pound, so Cameco expects more cash flow in 2025. The company also made the final repayment of $200 million in credit used to finance Westinghouse, and the loan is now fully paid. This reduced the company's debt burden to the current $1.3 billion. This good credit score is seen in the 5.88 Altman Z score, which is above 3, which means that the company has a strong going concern aspect and is not becoming insolvent in the near future.

I am also optimistic, following the strong uranium contract portfolio. The company closed Q4’24 and FY 2024 at 220 million pounds. The breakdown is 28 million lb/year between 2025 to 2029.

Valuation

From the financials above, I have noted that the company estimates EPS improvement, and analysts’ forecasts EPS projection reveals optimism. From this context, I value Cameco using cash flow from operations and its Price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio (EV/EBIT). This is amongst its competitors Uranium Energy Corp (NYSE: UEC), Energy Fuels Inc (NYSE: UUUU), and Centrus Energy Corp (NYSE: LEU).

Cash from operations shows the following:

This reveals that CCJ is leading amongst its competitors in cash generated from its operations. The difference from $629.68 million to the second company, LEU, at $37 million indicates Cameca's lead in selling Uranium.

Also, Cameco's P/E ratio will show whether its current price matches its earnings. Using EV/EBIT, the competitors demonstrate the following:

At the current dip, Cameco is not overvalued at a P/E ratio 52.75. Instead, the company is setting high expectations for its future growth. The high P/E ratio amongst its competitors shows investors are willing to buy its shares at a premium. This explains why the company has a positive EPS and projection of a double-digit EPS of $0.24 per common share by 2026, which shows the high expectation. This confirms why I am reiterating Cameco's optimism.

Investment risks

  • Geopolitical risks – Despite the ban on Russian Uranium from entering the US, the current tariffs by the US on Canada, China, and other listed nations are likely to halt Cameco operations.
  • Fluctuating currency exchange – the company could be adversely affected by changes in currency rates due to royalty rates, tariffs, inflation, and interest rates. This would affect the performance of company financials.
  • Regulatory risks – risks associated with 1the inability to enforce company legal rights under the current agreements, licenses, and permits may delay mining operations.

data.title: Rent the Runway, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Rent the Runway, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: DocuSign: Growth Should Start To Accelerate
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on DocuSign (NASDAQ:DOCU). My summarized thesis is that growth is finally showing signs of reaccelerating, driven by strong early traction from its IAM platform and a clear rebound in enterprise expansion. DOCU could realistically return to low-teens growth over the next few years starting in FY27, assuming continued execution on IAM adoption and enterprise expansion.

Company overview

DOCU is best known for its eSignature product, which enables organizations to send, sign, and manage agreements digitally in a secure and compliant manner. Over the years, DOCU has also expanded into adjacent verticals like Contract Lifecycle Management [CLM] and, more recently, Intelligent Agreement Management [IAM]. As of 4Q25, DOCU had ~1.7 million customers, including ~260,000 enterprise and commercial accounts. By revenue contribution, subscription is ~98% of total revenue.

Growth drivers

I believe DOCU is near its growth inflection point, with low-teens revenue growth in sight, and there are multiple visible growth drivers that should make this happen.

Firstly, it is the adoption of DOCU’s IAM platform (launched in April 2024). As a refresher, the IAM platform combines eSignature, workflow automation, AI-based contract insights, and a developer ecosystem, and this platform serves both enterprise and SMB. While it is still in its early phase, the adoption traction has been extremely solid. Per management, IAM was included in a high-single-digit percentage of all deals in 4Q25 and contributed >20% of direct sales during the quarter. Perhaps more notably, while IAM currently represents a low-single-digit [LSD] percentage of DOCU's recurring subscription revenue base, management has guided for this figure to grow to a low-double-digit [LDD] percentage by 4Q26. If we assume LSD is 3% and LDD is 11%, this basically implies a more than 3x increase in IAM’s revenue contribution within a year, and if the momentum holds, IAM itself would contribute ~3% to overall growth in FY27 (300% growth * 11% of total revenue).

What gives me confidence that this momentum can continue is the clear value IAM delivers to users. Unlike a standalone SKU, IAM includes a variety of tools that significantly improve productivity. For instance, Navigator (an AI-powered agreement repository) and Maestro (a no-code workflow engine) allow customers to automate time-consuming processes and be more efficient (able to use AI to extract better actionable insights). Essentially, IAM significantly reduces manual effort, speeds up execution, and helps organizations gain greater visibility and control over their agreement data. On top of the growth metrics, the adoption feedback from both mid-market and enterprise users is also very encouraging. Management even noted IAM as the fastest-growing new product in DOCU’s history, outpacing earlier introductions like CLM.

Secondly, DOCU’s enterprise expansion is picking up again. The data from the recent quarter is a major signal of this. In 4Q25, DOCU added 56 customers with annual contract value [ACV] greater than $300k, bringing the total to 1,131. This was not only a significant improvement over the nine net adds in 3Q25 but also marked the strongest quarter since 3Q23. This rebound in momentum is particularly encouraging on two fronts: (1) it suggests IAM is a very effective product that helps DOCU to land new large accounts and has the potential to unlock higher ACV as DOCU “expands” within these organizations; and (2) it suggests that DOCU has largely fixed the issues with its direct sales motion.

If this momentum continues, DOCU should see a meaningful acceleration in large-deal volume and expansion revenue. That, in turn, would directly support higher billings and revenue growth.

Lastly, while enterprise expansion and IAM are the key growth drivers, I believe self-service sales motion should not be overlooked. Remember that digital revenue still grows faster than overall revenue in 4Q25 (~26% y/y), and this momentum could strengthen further as IAM becomes available in formats better suited for smaller customers. These smaller customers, in particular, should benefit from productivity gains offered by the AI-powered features like Navigator and Maestro, as they typically do not have the resources to build custom workflows or dedicate headcount to manage contracts manually. IAM allows them to automate and streamline key agreement processes in a way that’s both scalable and cost-efficient. Besides, unlike large enterprise deals, self-service sales typically require minimal sales support, so DOCU could scale rapidly without spending a lot on headcount or marketing.

Momentum Is Clearly Turning

Several quantitative data points from the recent 4Q25 results also further support my view that growth is set to accelerate. Total revenue grew 9% y/y to $776.3 million, which is a notable acceleration from the past few quarters ~7% y/y growth rate. Billings growth also accelerated to 11% y/y vs. 8.8% y/y in 3Q25. There was also an uptick in net dollar retention [NDR], which came in at 101% vs. 98% in 4Q24. This may not seem like a large move, but it breaks a multi-quarter downtrend and suggests improving expansion behavior within the customer base. Notably, the fact that DOCU is seeing early signs of reacceleration—at the same time that IAM attach rates are increasing—implies that IAM adoption is translating into tangible improvements financially.

Valuation

I am expecting DOCU to accelerate its growth back to low teens over the coming few years, with FY26 being the trough (management guided to 6% y/y growth). Assuming a linear growth acceleration over 3 years, I expect DOCU to generate ~$4.2 billion in revenue. I am not assuming any margin expansion, as DOCU most likely needs to reinvest aggressively into its IAM platform to make it more relevant (e.g., more AI products). It is also a conservative assumption that leaves more room for upside surprises. Say margin stays at 25%; DOCU should generate ~$1 billion in adjusted net income in FY29. With a recovery back to low-teens growth and margin staying flat, I would imagine DOCU multiples to see mean reversion—back to 23x forward PE—from the current ~21x. This translates to a share price target of $118 in FY29, or an upside of 60% over 4 years.

Investment Risk

While momentum is improving, it is still a question mark around IAM adoption at scale, especially as monetization is still early. In other words, if DOCU starts to ramp up monetization, it may result in lower adoption, which will limit the growth potential. Additionally, the current macro environment is not at its best. If things go south—a recession happens—we may see more businesses pull back/delay tech investments, which will push back the timing of DOCU’s growth accelerating back to low teens.

Conclusion

My positive view on DOCU is because the company is showing early signs that growth is turning. IAM adoption is scaling very well, large-deal activity is recovering, and digital channels remain a growth catalyst. Notably, these dynamics are translating into tangible improvements—billings growth, net retention, and revenue trends are all pointing in the right direction. If this continues, I believe DOCU can rerate meaningfully as the market begins to price in a return to low-teens revenue growth.

data.title: Intel Has More Going For It Than You Think
data.description:
Intel Corp. (NASDAQ:INTC) has been a rotten investment for quite some time, with a steady decline in price, after missing out on the development and sales of smartphone and AI chips experiencing hot demand over the last 3-5 years. These failures have been well documented and reflected in the stock price/valuation.

However, there is much to like about Intel in early 2025. This investment is a classic "sum of the parts" breakup story. With new CEO Lip-Bu Tan hired weeks ago and heat on the board of directors for real change at the company, I suspect 2025 will be a year to remember for all stakeholders.

My last bullish article in January here, highlighted the potential for a share turnaround in coming months. Measured against a still high AI-inspired valuation for the entire chip sector, Intel is definitely a contrarian, against-the-grain choice.

Believe it or not, Intel's stock quote has been morphing into an "outperformance" pick in early 2025, and could be a top semiconductor pick this year on asset shuffles.

Let me explain why I still own a decent position size in Intel, alongside the logic for a continued Buy rating.

Divestitures and Change of Direction

For starters, this company is seen as one of the top beneficiaries of President Trump's tariff effort to reshore domestic semiconductor manufacturing capacity. A concerted push into foundry building inside the U.S. (especially with help from federal government tax incentives, low-interest rate loans, and actual tax credits under President Biden through the CHIPS and Science Act of 2022), has been a giant money losing decision so far.

Over the weekend, Trump has been hinting at a 25% tariff rate for semiconductors produced in Asia, including Taiwan, perhaps enacted in another month or two. If such takes place, profit incentives to send orders to American-based fabs, the same ones Intel is constructing, could become reality.

The good news is rumors of a spinoff or sale of a portion of this division have been picking up steam since January. The main rumor for Foundry division assets is Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) will take the management role in a newly formed entity with a 20% ownership stake, while other fabless chip leaders like NVIDIA (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Broadcom (AVGO), buy smaller stakes in the business. The idea is Intel is a direct competitor with many fabless entities. So, if Intel remained a partial owner, but did not have direct access to outside patented designs and processes, America's top semi names would be willing to get their product from such a creation. Intel's 100% ownership and management of the Foundry division has been the key sticking point for outside orders. Makes sense to me.

In related news, it was reported during early March most of these chip designers were testing Intel's new 18A manufacturing process for semiconductor speed, efficiency and accuracy.

With Intel's Foundry losing the most money of any division (negative $11.2 billion for operating income in 2024), sharing the losses now as additional fabrication facilities come online (and any future profits), on top of getting potentially $15-20 billion in cash for selling 50% to 60% of ownership in the assets (my estimate based on carried asset values over the last four years of heavy capital spending), would be a transformational transaction for shareholder value.

In addition, Intel owns important stakes in other divisions enjoying little profitability during 2024. Rumors of substantial interest in Mobileye (MBLY) here have been floating around for years. Intel was a majority owner of 87.7% of Mobileye equity and 98.6% of voting power at the end of December 2024, with a market value to Intel of $9.5 billion using MBLY's $13 quote. Mobileye is the leading maker of hardware and software for autonomous, self-driving cars on the road today.

In fact, this morning (April 14th) Intel announced it was selling 51% of Altera (programmable chip unit) to private equity firm Silver Lake for $4.4 billion to help reduce debt and fund Foundry CAPEX this year. Keeping the other 49% as an investment in the future and possible liquidity later down the line leaves Intel's financial options open.

Also, during April Intel did raise $1.9 billion selling its NAND memory technology and manufacturing business to the South Korean tech giant SK hynix.

When you add up all of the possible unit sales, I come up with numbers between $30-40 billion in potential cash infused into the core Intel business. Such could allow the company to both slash non-core operating losses, while paying down most all of its debt (now bearing an interest expense) standing at a net $28 billion at the end of December ($22 billion in cash vs. $50 billion in total debt).

At that point, after the transactions have closed later in 2025 into 2026, Intel could be a highly profitable enterprise again, benefiting from Trump tariffs, with a large foundry investment earning serious bucks by 2027. Getting back into the neighborhood of 2020's $5 EPS high number would not be impossible by 2030.

Current Valuation Stats

For a turnaround situation in a large blue chip, investors should (to a degree) place less emphasis on immediate income generation. New product rollouts, cost cutting, asset sales and the like can radically alter the profit picture several years out.

For sure, the earnings outlook for this business as configured before April's asset transactions was not incredibly rosy. Analyst estimates were calling for flattish revenue totals with a normalized P/E closer to 17x in 2026 and 12x in 2027.

Instead, I like to focus on foundational asset values and repeatable revenue generation. In particular, price to tangible book value and sales (until revenue-producing assets are divested) calculations can shed the brightest light on underlying company worth.

On price to tangible book value (the net liquidation accounting value, based on cost accounting and the depreciation of assets, minus all liabilities), Intel is today selling for basically its lowest valuation ever at 1.2x tangible BV. The long-term median yearly average is about 4.5x for this financial analysis stat, going back to 1985.

Below I am comparing INTC to the biggest semiconductor businesses you can own in the stock market by equity capitalization, with a tangible book value in early 2025. The sort group includes Advanced Micro Devices, Qualcomm, NVIDIA, Micron (MU), Texas Instruments (TXN), ARM Holdings (ARM), Tawan Semi, Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY), Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), ASE Technology (ASX), and Global Foundries (GFS). Intel is easily the cheapest on the list. The median average is now above 6x.

For the time being, price to sales of 1.59x is approaching the lowest level since late 1988. The long-term average ratio for the business is just under 4x. In addition, the peer/competitor group currently sports a median average P/S ratio above 4x.

The conclusion is INTC holds considerable underlying value to long-term investors, if material earnings do return, one way or another.

Final Thoughts

Q1 2025 earnings are scheduled for release on April 24th. We should get more information on operating activities and asset divestitures at that date. Last quarter's results easily beat Wall Street expectations, but management guidance for the first three months of this year were quite lackluster.

What are the short-term risks owning Intel? I feel they are macroeconomic in nature if a U.S. (and perhaps global) recession is beginning. I would surmise share price gains will be difficult to limited in a deep recession scenario during 2025. In addition, a continuation of Wall Street price weakness in a bear market move could also hold Intel in check into the summertime. Lastly, Asian nations retaliating against U.S. tariffs could hurt Intel chip sales moving into computer and electronics supply chains.

Yet, Intel has the potential to raise $30 to $40 billion in assets divestitures and move to a uniquely strong net-cash position on its balance sheet over time, from a growing net-debt position in 2024. The new CEO is focused on reinvigorating its core, remaining chip development and attractiveness for Big Tech OEMs. He wants to hire the best engineers available and restore Intel's reputation as an innovator.

So, today's $86 billion equity market cap looks quite desirable to own at $20 per share. Operating losses at the Foundry division could be cut dramatically (and shared with others), while still keeping a large ownership stake in ramping domestic U.S. chip production.

And, if executed properly, the remaining business could be generating upwards of $13.5 billion in after-tax income annually by 2027 ($2 billion as the current analyst 2025 forecast + $500 million in cash interest benefit +$11 billion in eliminated operating losses at its money losing divisions). Don't say it cannot happen.

Using an estimated future enterprise value as low as $75-85 billion on the current $20 share quote, patient Intel buyers could be getting a top semiconductor firm for less than 6x after-tax income on a cash/debt neutral, take-private calculation, in the not-too-distant future. (My assumptions include an operating free cash flow shortfall of $10-15 billion to fund expected 2025 CAPEX for new foundries, before any partnership plays out.)

This ultra-low 6x number compares to the Big Tech semiconductor names going for a median average of 25x EV to earnings in April 2025. In other words, Intel "could" be priced at a 70%+ discount to future trading, using a best-case bullish scenario of the stock quote headed toward $75 over 3-4 years. (This would assume the Trump trade-war effort calms down or is reversed by the summer.)

Food for thought anyway, if you believe Intel can only lose money from operations and slide closer to zero for a share price. That's also why investors should be forward looking in their investment analysis of a left-for-dead blue chip with plenty of avenues to increase shareholder value. Rearview mirror watching may talk you into missing out on a huge investment gainer over the next 24-36 months.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Tesco: Upgrade To Buy On Improved Fundamentals And Cheaper Valuation
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDY) in Oct’24, which I downgraded to a hold rating due to my concerns about the valuation being too high, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I have upgraded my rating from hold to buy as valuation has become cheaper despite the fundamentals improving, which I now see as a more attractive risk-reward setup.

Investment thesis

A few days ago, TSCDY released its FY25 earnings, which demonstrated strong execution on cost management. Total sales came in at GBP69.9 billion, and while y/y growth was only 1.5%, the highlight was the growth seen at the adj EBIT level. Total adj. EBIT came in at GBP3.13 billion, which marked a 7.6% y/y growth and margin expansion of 25% bps y/y. As a result, underlying net income grew 8.6% y/y to GBP1.89 billion, or adjusted EPS of 27.36 pence. What makes the FY26 setup great is that management seems to be guiding very conservatively. They guided to an adj. EBIT of GBP2.7–3 billion, which implies a decline from FY25 levels.

While this never-growth guide seems negative at face value, I think this is a tactical move. This guidance would mean that TSCDY has reset market expectations, giving it more flexibility to double down on its pricing strategy to protect its market position (or potentially gain share) during a market downturn. While some may think that raising prices to protect profit margins is the right thing to do, I think protecting market share—both volume share and consumer mind share—is the key to long-term value creation.

The reality is that consumers tend to stay loyal to the brands that “helped” them when times were tough, and I believe management clearly understands this. In FY25, over 2,300 products were cheaper at the end of the year than at the start, with an average reduction of around 9%. These results were apparent as food like-for-like [LFL] sales grew 4.9% y/y in the UK—implying solid traffic growth as pricing was down. At a time when consumers continue to be value conscious, TSCDY's intention to remain the cheapest full-line grocer, along with further improvements in price positioning since January 2025, gives me confidence it will continue gaining share.

If there were any doubts about TSCDY customer traction, the numbers from 4Q25 should put them to rest. UK market share increased by 67 bps y/y to 28.3%, reaching its highest level since 2016. It has also now delivered 21 consecutive four-week periods of market share gains. Notably, volume grew in every quarter, and large-store LFL sales increased 4.1% y/y. Even online momentum was strong, with weekly online orders up 10.8% y/y and Tesco Whoosh—TSCDY rapid delivery service—nearly doubling its sales. Management also specifically called out that customer satisfaction reached a five-year high, reinforcing my bullish view that TSCDY pricing strategies are resonating.

Importantly, as TSCDY pulls in more customer traffic, its scale advantage only widens — allowing it to spread fixed costs over a broader base, sustain its pricing strategy, and still protect margins. That’s a dynamic that smaller peers will struggle to keep up with.

Another aspect of TSCDY business that deserves attention is its retail media and data monetization efforts. While we do not know the exact revenue and profit contribution, we know that this business is scaling up very quickly. For reference, in FY25, TSCDY delivered >9,000 retail media campaigns and scaled up its media formats to include video advertising across Tesco.com and the mobile app. It also deployed >5,000 in-store digital screens and expanded into store-wrap advertising at scale. These are very high-margin businesses that I think, with TSCDY's increased scale, make it even more attractive to advertisers that are looking to target value-conscious consumers during this tough time.

We know that these efforts are resonating with advertisers from two data points. First is that management noted supplier satisfaction was at record highs, and participation levels had returned to pre-COVID levels. This is extremely significant, because it means brands are not only buying into TSCDY’s platform, they are also spending more per campaign. Secondly, TSCDY’s platform was ranked joint #1 by Flywheel among European retail media networks, and it was shortlisted for Media Brand of the Year.

Tesco Media and Insight Platform saw growth in active advertisers and spend per campaign, with the team ranked joint #1 in Flywheel's European retail media rankings and shortlisted for Media Brand of the Year at the Media Week Awards. FY25 earnings results call

These qualitative signals suggest that this aspect of TSCDY business could increasingly become a material contributor to earnings over the medium term. It also gives confidence that TSCDY can be even more aggressive on its pricing strategy, as this source of revenue has very high margins.

Valuation

Unlike in October last year, TSCDY valuation has been de-rated down to 11.6x NTM PE (from ~13x) (note the past 5-year average is 12.4x). At the current multiple, I think the upside is attractive as the market is not pricing in aggressive earnings growth. I believe TSCDY will see its growth accelerate back to mid-single-digits over the coming years, backed by an increased scale, better customer loyalty, and contribution from its retail media business. I am being conservative about the margin outlook, with a belief that TSCDY will continue to maintain its pricing strategy, but if retail media margins are way better than expected, margins could go up (and this is an upside potential). I am also not assuming multiples to expand back to 13x. I simply assume multiples to see mean reversion, and if all these play out, I see an upside of 18%.

Risk

The key risk to TSCDY is irrational pricing competition in the UK grocery market. Peers could be forced to cut prices aggressively to clear inventory (better to mark down to reduce cost than to completely write off inventory), and TSCDY may lose volume share. Additionally, wage inflation and national insurance increases (around GBP235 million impact) could weigh on operating leverage. Lastly, slower-than-expected monetization of retail media or digital platforms may put a lid on how fast growth can accelerate.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for TSCDY is upgraded to buy. TSCDY has delivered solid execution, and I believe it is able to continue defending its market position. Its pricing strategy and traction in its retail media business led me to believe that growth can accelerate to mid-single-digits eventually. I don’t think the current valuation fully reflects these drivers, and if my assumptions are right, I see upside potential of 18%.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
